Regeneron beats expectations on Dupixent strength despite pressure on Eylea
data.description:
The 28% drop in Eylea sales, to $1.1bn, was partly due to delays in production validation processes. Three filings remain pending FDA approval, although the agency has cleared a new site for packaging the drug's high-dose version, with a decision expected by mid-2027. Meanwhile, Libtayo, Regeneron's cancer treatment, generated $425m, far exceeding expectations.

For the quarter as a whole, the drugmaker's revenue reached $3.88bn, versus $3.80bn expected. EPS reached $11.44, also above forecasts ($10.62). Investors remain cautious about 2031 however,, when several key Dupixent patents are set to expire, and about ongoing talks with the Trump administration over drug price regulation, which could weigh on the sector's future profitability.

On Friday morning the stock was up in New York following these announcements.

data.title: Disney Hires Morgan Stanley's Swinburne to Lead IR, Corporate Strategy
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher


Disney has hired Morgan Stanley analyst Benjamin Swinburne as the entertainment giant's new executive vice president of investor relations and corporate strategy.

Disney on Friday said Swinburne, most recently managing director and head of U.S. media and telecom & cable services research at Morgan Stanley, will join the Burbank, Calif., company in the near future.

Swinburne, who joined Morgan Stanley as a research analyst in 1999, will report directly to Hugh Johnston, Disney's chief financial officer, the company said.

Disney said Swinburne will lead communication with its investors, sell-side analysts and other key stakeholders and will also oversee the company's long-term strategic planning and market analysis, identifying growth opportunities based on industry trends and evolving entertainment consumption.


Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-30-26 0945ET

India's real-estate firm Confident Group founder C.J. Roy dies by suicide, local police said
data.description:
Jan 30 (Reuters) - C.J. Roy, the founder and chair of real-estate firm Confident Group, died by suicide in the southern city of Bengaluru on Friday, local police said.

Roy shot himself and was pronounced dead at a nearby hospital, Bengaluru city police chief Seemanth Kumar Singh told the media in a briefing.

Initial reports suggest that there was an ongoing tax raid, Singh said, without disclosing further details.

Authorities were conducting income tax raids against the group in Bengaluru when Roy shot himself and was rushed to Narayana Hospital, several Indian media outlets said.

Founded nearly two decades ago, Confident Group is one of the top property developers in India's southern state of Kerala as well as in Bengaluru, according to its website.

Reuters could not immediately contact the company, and authorities at Narayana Hospital were not immediately available for comment.

(Reporting by Komal Salecha and Brijesh Patel in Bengaluru; writing by Abinaya Vijayaraghavan; Editing by Anil D'Silva)

Euro zone economy ends 2025 with solid growth despite challenges
data.description:
STORY: The euro zone economy grew quicker than expected last quarter.

That's what official data showed on Friday (January 30).

It rose as consumption and investments sped up.

That momentum offset low exports and the unceratinty caused by U.S. trade policy.

The figures showed the bloc's resilience.

It was expected to struggle due to a trade war with the U.S, competition from China and years of conflict in Ukraine.

But the euro zone produced moderate growth each quarter last year.

The bloc's economy grew by 0.3% in the fourth quarter - slightly ahead of analyst projections.

It was also up 1.3% compared with a year earlier.

Spain was still the driver of growth as it jumped a quicker-than-forecast 0.8%.

Germany saw slight expansion for its best quarterly performance in three years.

Elsewhere, industry showed signs of stabilization.

While unemployment held near record lows and inflation was steady around the European Central Bank's 2% target.

However, exports aren't likely to recover fully anytime soon due to U.S. tariffs and tough Chinese competition.
